canto_title: The Descent into the Valley of Violence and the Encounter with the Centaurs

scenes.summary: Dante describes the massive rocky descent caused by a landslide and the sight of the Minotaur, which bites itself in rage upon seeing the travelers.
Virgil commands the beast to depart, clarifying that they are not there to be taught by the Minotaur's kin but to witness the suffering of the damned.
Dante observes the Minotaur's unstable, leaping movements and Virgil warns him to take cover while the beast is in its fury.
The two travelers begin their descent down the unstable, shifting stones of the precipice.
Virgil explains that the great rock fell due to the seismic shock caused by the arrival of a certain powerful figure in the upper circles.
Virgil directs Dante's eyes toward the approaching river of boiling blood where the victims of violence are immersed.
Dante pauses to reflect on the destructive nature of greed and anger in human life and eternity.
The travelers see a wide ditch where a group of centaurs, armed with arrows, hunt like hunters in the world.
Three centaurs part from the group and threaten to shoot the travelers if they do not reveal their purpose.
Virgil instructs Dante to remain quiet and wait to answer the centaurs up close.
Virgil identifies the centaurs as Nessus, Chiron, and Pholus.
Dante observes the mass of centaurs continuously shooting arrows into the trench.
The travelers approach the creatures, and Chiron uses an arrow to pull back his lips and inspect them.
Chiron questions his companions, wondering if someone is hidden behind the travelers to manipulate them.
Virgil responds that they are not thieves and explains they must traverse this path by the force of divine virtue, requesting the centaur to carry them.
Chiron instructs Nessus to lead the travelers and guide them safely through the valley.
The travelers move along the banks of the red, boiling river where the souls of the violent scream.
Chiron identifies the tyrants submerged in the blood, including Alexander the Great and various Italian lords.
Dante turns to Virgil, who tells him that he will be the first to learn these things and Dante will be second.
Chiron stops by a mass of souls, pointing out a figure whose blood flows into the Thames.
Dante observes more souls whose heads and bodies are partially submerged in the river.
The level of the blood rises so high that it begins to touch the travelers' feet as they walk along the edge.
Chiron explains that the bottom of the trench descends deeper to where even greater tyranny is punished.
Chiron describes how divine justice punishes figures like Attila and the Rinvieri, whose tears are harvested by the boiling blood.
Chiron turns away and returns to the depths of the river.
